title: Cost of Moving to the Netherlands
metaTitle: Cost of Moving to the Netherlands: Budget, Expenses, and What to Plan For
subtitle: What to budget for when relocating: travel, documents, housing, shipping, and your first month of setup.
description: How much does it cost to move to the Netherlands? See typical relocation expenses, budget examples, housing and document costs, and how to plan your move.

quickAnswers.label: What this page shows
quickAnswers.value: Typical cost areas when moving to the Netherlands, example planning ranges, and what many expats underestimate.

quickAnswers.label: What costs typically include
quickAnswers.value: Travel, visa/document preparation (apostilles, translations), temporary and first rental (deposit + rent), shipping or luggage, and first-month setup (transport, SIM, groceries, basics).

quickAnswers.label: What amounts depend on
quickAnswers.value: Distance and how you travel, whether you need visas or document prep, household size and housing, whether you ship goods or travel light, and city or area.

sections.heading: Overview
body: The cost of moving to the Netherlands depends on your origin country, visa route, household size, housing choices, and whether you ship belongings. This guide explains the main relocation cost categories, typical budget ranges, and what many expats underestimate when planning their move.

All figures on this page are indicative planning ranges only. Actual costs vary. We do not give specific official fees unless we have a vetted source; we focus on what to plan for and where costs tend to vary.

links.label: Documents needed to move to the Netherlands

sections.heading: Estimated relocation costs
body: These are broad example ranges for planning only. Actual costs vary by origin country, visa route, family size, housing choices, and whether you travel light or relocate with shipping.

summaryBox.title: Typical total relocation budget
summaryBox.note: Solo EU moves can fall below this range; family or non-EU relocations can exceed it.

sections.heading: Main cost categories
body: Below are the main cost areas to plan for. Each varies by situation; we have linked to guides that go into more detail where relevant.
bullets: Flights and travel — One-way or relocation travel costs depend on origin country, timing, and baggage. See: Moving timeline.
Documents and visas — Can include certificates, translations, apostilles, and permit-related admin. See: Documents needed to move.
Housing — Usually the largest cost area: deposit, first rent, and temporary accommodation. See: Moving with family, Documents needed, timeline.
Shipping and luggage — From travelling light (€0) to international movers; high variability by volume and route.
First month setup — Transport, SIM, groceries, basic household items, and one-off setup costs.
Emergency buffer — Many expats keep €1,000 – €3,000 aside for delays, extra admin, or unexpected setup costs.

sections.heading: Emergency buffer
body: Many expats keep an additional buffer for delays, temporary housing changes, extra admin, or setup costs.

summaryBox.title: Suggested range
summaryBox.note: Some people need less; others prefer a larger buffer if they are relocating with family or arriving without housing confirmed.

sections.heading: What people often underestimate
body: Deposit and first rent together often add up to two or three months' rent; some landlords also ask for proof of income or a higher deposit for newcomers.

Temporary housing before securing long-term housing can stretch the budget, especially in popular cities. Document translations and apostilles can take weeks and add up, especially if you need several certificates. Registration-related delays can mean extra nights in temporary accommodation or rush fees. Extra baggage or shipping often costs more than people expect. For families, travel, documents, deposit, and first-month setup all multiply.
Below are two scenario callouts to keep in mind.

personaExample.title: Solo EU move
personaExample.text: Typically lower document and visa costs; focus on travel, deposit, first rent, and first-month setup. A small buffer helps if registration or banking takes longer. Housing choice is often the biggest swing factor.

callout.title: Non-EU and family moves
callout.text: Non-EU moves usually involve more document prep (apostilles, translations, permit fees) and sometimes proof of funds. Family moves multiply travel, documents, deposit, first rent, and often shipping; school and childcare may add to initial setup. See our guides on moving with family and EU vs non-EU moves.

links.label: Moving to the Netherlands with family
EU vs non-EU moving to the Netherlands

sections.heading: Ways to reduce or manage costs
body: Practical steps that often help keep relocation costs under control:
bullets: Start document preparation early to avoid rush fees — use the Document Readiness tool to see what applies.
Compare temporary housing and confirm whether it allows registration.
Travel lighter if possible and compare baggage vs shipping.
Spread setup spending over your first month where possible.
Keep a checklist so you do not miss steps that create extra costs — use the Moving Checklist Generator.
Budget deposit and first rent before optimizing smaller costs.

sections.heading: Typical cost areas and variability
body: How much each cost area tends to vary, and why. Use this to prioritise what you research and budget for.

sections.heading: Planning checklist
body: Use this list to make sure you have budgeted for the main areas that apply to your move.
bullets: List all document and visa-related costs for your route.
Budget for deposit and at least first month's rent.
Plan travel and luggage or shipping.
Include a buffer for first-month setup and delays.
Use the First 90 Days planner to sequence post-arrival steps.

faq.q: How much money do I need to move to the Netherlands?
faq.a: There is no single number; it depends on your route, household, and city. Typical total relocation budgets range from around €3,000 to €12,000 or more. Solo EU moves can fall below that; family or non-EU relocations often exceed it. Plan for travel, documents, deposit and first rent, first-month living costs, and a buffer. Use the Moving Checklist and Document Readiness tools to list what applies to you.

faq.q: What are the main cost categories when relocating?
faq.a: Common categories are: travel, visa/document preparation, temporary and first housing (deposit + rent), shipping or luggage, and first-month setup (transport, SIM, groceries, basics). We describe these in more detail on this page and link to the Moving checklist and Documents needed guides.

faq.q: Do costs differ for EU vs non-EU moves?
faq.a: Often yes. Non-EU moves usually involve visa/permit fees and more document preparation (apostilles, translations). Housing and first-month costs depend on household and city rather than nationality. See our EU vs non-EU moving guide for more.

faq.q: Is housing usually the biggest moving cost?
faq.a: For many people, yes. Deposit (often one to three months' rent) plus first month's rent and possibly temporary accommodation before you secure a long-term place can add up to the largest share of relocation costs. Budget for housing first, then other categories.

faq.q: Should I budget an emergency buffer?
faq.a: Many expats do. A typical suggested range is €1,000 – €3,000 for delays, temporary housing changes, extra admin, or setup costs. Some need less; others prefer a larger buffer if relocating with family or arriving without housing confirmed.
